scenes.title: Hook: The Business We've Been Overlooking
scenes.narration: Hey. So I want to show you something. Not a pitch deck, not a spreadsheet. Just a real look at a business we could actually start, that local shops and service providers would genuinely pay for, and that we can deliver without losing our minds.
scenes.visual_prompt: Deep cinematic gradient background shifting from near-black navy to rich midnight blue. Centered on screen: a single glowing microphone icon with a soft golden halo radiating outward. Below it, a subtle grid of tiny local business icons fades into the dark background — a coffee cup, a dental cross, a wrench, a barbell. No readable text in the image itself. Mood: intimate, premium, quietly exciting. Finish: matte with a faint film grain overlay.
scenes.staging_notes: Open with a slow zoom toward the microphone icon. Business icons in the background should feel like a subtle reveal — they're there if you look, but the mic is the hero. Warm and inviting, not corporate.
on_screen_text: A business idea worth a real conversation.

scenes.title: The Simple Pitch: What We're Actually Doing
scenes.narration: Here's the whole idea in one breath. Local businesses need video ads. They can't afford agencies. We use voice cloning and a tuned production pipeline to make premium narrated commercials fast and cheap. They pay us. We deliver in days. That's it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean motion beat on a deep charcoal background. Three horizontal steps appear one by one from left to right, each in a glowing pill shape: Step one in amber gold, Step two in electric teal, Step three in soft white. Minimalist sans-serif labels beneath each pill. Confident, modern, fast-paced reveal.
scenes.staging_notes: Stage as a three-beat sequential reveal. Each pill animates in with a quick pop and a subtle glow pulse. Hold on all three for a beat before the narration wraps. Keep it punchy — this is the orientation moment.

scenes.title: The Problem They're Already Paying to Ignore
scenes.narration: Here's what's wild. Most local businesses know they need video content. They just can't afford an agency, don't have time to DIY it, and don't trust a freelancer they found at midnight. So they do nothing. Or they post a blurry phone video and hope for the best.
scenes.visual_prompt: Split composition on a warm dark gray background. Left half: a glossy agency invoice prop graphic showing a large blurred dollar figure with the word 'Agency' at the top, soft red accent. Right half: a grainy low-quality phone screenshot aesthetic, slightly tilted, suggesting a bad DIY video. A bold diagonal slash separates the two halves in bright amber. Lighting: dramatic single overhead source. Mood: wry, knowing, a little funny.
scenes.staging_notes: The two panels should feel like an unflattering before photo — but with a knowing, slightly comedic tone. Not mean-spirited. Just honest.

scenes.title: Enter the Pipeline: How We Solve It
scenes.narration: We built a pipeline that handles the heavy lifting. Client fills out a short brief. We generate a script, clone or assign a commercial voice, pair it with visuals, and deliver a finished narrated video. The whole thing can run in a day or two. No studio. No crew. No back-and-forth nightmare.
scenes.visual_prompt: Horizontal workflow diagram on a deep navy background. Five connected nodes in a clean left-to-right flow: a clipboard icon, a text document icon, a waveform microphone icon, a film frame icon, and a checkmark badge icon. Each node connected by a glowing teal animated-style arrow. Icons are flat but given depth with soft drop shadows and subtle inner glow. No readable text inside icons. Mood: efficient, modern, quietly impressive. Premium matte finish.
scenes.staging_notes: Nodes should feel like a pipeline — clean and connected. The teal arrows are the story: this is a flow, not a one-off. Overlay the text as a single clean line beneath the diagram.

scenes.title: The Voices: A Recurring Cast of Characters
scenes.narration: Now here's where it gets interesting. We're not using one generic voice for everything. We have a small cast of cloned commercial voices — each tuned for a different vibe. There's Marcus, who sounds like he sells trucks and means it. There's Jenna, warm and trustworthy, perfect for healthcare. And there's Coop, who makes a gym sound like the best decision of your life.
scenes.visual_prompt: Three vertical character cards on a dark gradient background, each with a distinct color accent. Left card in deep amber for Marcus, center card in soft teal for Jenna, right card in electric blue for Coop. Each card shows a stylized audio waveform beneath the name. Clean, modern card layout with subtle glow borders.
scenes.staging_notes: Cards should animate in sequentially — Marcus, then Jenna, then Coop — each with a quick slide-up and fade. Hold all three on screen together for the final beat. Bella's narration drives the reveal timing. Keep it fun and a little theatrical.

scenes.title: Example: The Auto Shop Ad (Marcus)
scenes.narration: Tired of getting the runaround at the dealership? At Riverside Auto Care, we do honest work at honest prices. From oil changes to full engine rebuilds — our ASE-certified technicians treat your car like it's their own. Call us today, or stop by the shop on Fifth. We'll take it from there.
scenes.visual_prompt: Cinematic wide-angle scene: the interior of a clean, well-lit auto repair bay. A lifted pickup truck on a hydraulic lift in the center frame. Warm industrial overhead lighting casting dramatic shadows on polished concrete. A mechanic's silhouette works in the background. In the foreground, a blurred tool chest with gleaming chrome accents adds depth. Background environment is rich amber and steel gray. Mood: capable, trustworthy, blue-collar pride. No text elements in the image.
scenes.staging_notes: This is a demo moment — show what a real client ad sounds like. Marcus is the voice. Overlay the business name and tagline as a clean lower-third style treatment. Premium but grounded.
on_screen_text: ASE-Certified · Call or Visit · Fifth Street

scenes.title: What Auto Shops Actually Pay Today
scenes.narration: So what does an auto shop spend on advertising today? Radio spots run anywhere from five hundred to fifteen hundred dollars just to produce, before the airtime. A local TV spot? Think two to five thousand. We can deliver a polished narrated video ad for a fraction of that — and they own it forever.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean comparison motion beat on dark charcoal background. Three price comparison rows stacked vertically, each with a category label on the left and a cost range on the right. Alternating subtle row highlights in deep navy and charcoal. A bold accent line in amber separates the competitor rows from our pricing row at the bottom.
scenes.staging_notes: Reveal rows one at a time, top to bottom. The 'Our Narrated Video Ad' row should land last with a visual emphasis — maybe a soft amber glow on the row and a subtle checkmark icon. The contrast should feel satisfying, not smug.
data_points: Our Video Ad: Significantly less — and they keep it forever
on_screen_text: Our Narrated Video Ad: Fraction of that. They own it.

scenes.title: Example: The Dental Practice Ad (Jenna)
scenes.narration: At Sunnyside Family Dental, we know a trip to the dentist isn't exactly anyone's favorite plan. That's why Dr. Patel and her team work hard to make every visit feel calm, comfortable, and actually worth your time. New patients welcome. Evening appointments available. Because your smile deserves a team that takes it seriously.
scenes.visual_prompt: Bright, airy dental office reception area rendered in soft editorial style. Warm white walls, a curved reception desk in pale oak, a single healthy green plant in the corner. Soft natural light filtering through frosted windows creates a gentle glow. The mood is calm and reassuring, clinical without being cold. A stethoscope and a small framed 'New Patients Welcome' sign visible on the desk as props. No readable text in the background. Mood: warm trust, professional calm.
scenes.staging_notes: Jenna's ad. Overlay as a soft lower-third — lighter typography to match the airy visual tone. This is the healthcare vertical demo moment. Keep it warm and credible.
on_screen_text: New Patients Welcome · Evening Hours Available

scenes.title: Example: The Gym Ad (Coop)
scenes.narration: Look — you don't need a perfect plan to start. You just need to show up. Iron District Gym has early morning classes, late-night open gym, and coaches who actually remember your name. First week is free. No contracts, no guilt trips, no nonsense. Come see what showing up feels like.
scenes.visual_prompt: Dramatic wide-angle gym interior shot. Rows of barbells and kettlebells gleaming under high-contrast industrial pendant lighting. Rubber flooring with subtle texture depth. A lone athlete in silhouette stands at the far end of the frame, facing away, creating a powerful sense of aspiration and scale. Deep shadows on the left, warm amber light on the right. High contrast, motivational, cinematic. No readable text in the environment.
scenes.staging_notes: Coop's ad. This one should feel punchy and kinetic even as a still. Overlay text in bold, high-contrast white with a subtle amber accent. The tagline is the emotional core.
on_screen_text: Show up. That's the whole plan.
First Week Free · No Contracts · Open Late

scenes.title: The Verticals: How Many Doors This Opens
scenes.narration: And here's the thing — it's not just auto shops, dentists, and gyms. Almost every local service business has the same problem and the same budget gap. We're talking plumbers, real estate agents, med spas, restaurants, insurance brokers, pet groomers. Every single one of them needs a voice and a video they can actually use.
scenes.visual_prompt: Animated grid of local business category icons on a deep midnight blue background. Twelve icons arranged in a three-by-four grid, each in its own softly glowing tile. Icons represent: wrench, tooth, barbell, house, stethoscope, fork and knife, dog paw, car, dollar sign, scissors, leaf, and briefcase. Each tile fades in sequentially. Clean, modern, icon-forward design.
scenes.staging_notes: Grid tiles should pop in one at a time in reading order — left to right, top to bottom. Final frame holds the full twelve. Bella's narration should pace naturally against the reveal. This is the 'we have a lot of doors to knock on' moment — it should feel abundant, not overwhelming.

scenes.title: The Math: What One Client Looks Like
scenes.narration: Let's talk numbers for a second. Say we charge five hundred dollars per video. A single client who orders two videos a month is a thousand dollars. Ten clients like that? That's ten thousand a month. And with the pipeline we've built, fulfillment time per video is measured in hours, not days.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean financial projection motion beat on a dark charcoal background. Three rows stacked vertically with large, clear numbers. A subtle upward-trending arrow motif in glowing teal runs behind the numbers. Sans-serif typography, bold and legible. Amber accent for the totals row.
scenes.staging_notes: Reveal one row at a time. The ten-client row should land with a bit of weight — maybe a subtle scale-up animation on the number. The fulfillment line lands last as the kicker. Keep the tone matter-of-fact, not hype-y. The numbers speak.

scenes.title: Why Voice Cloning Changes the Equation
scenes.narration: The thing that makes this actually scalable is voice cloning. Once Marcus, Jenna, and Coop are trained and tuned, they can narrate any script we write — instantly, consistently, at zero marginal cost. No studio bookings. No talent fees. No scheduling. The voice is always ready when we are.
scenes.visual_prompt: Abstract technology visualization on a deep navy to black gradient background. Center frame: a glowing circular waveform — like a voice iris — rendered in layered concentric rings of electric teal and amber. The rings pulse outward from a central point, suggesting broadcast and replication. Around the outer ring, three small labeled nodes glow softly: one in amber, one in teal, one in cool white. No readable text in the waveform itself. Mood: intelligent, modern, slightly futuristic but grounded. Premium finish with subtle lens flare.
scenes.staging_notes: The waveform should feel alive even as a still — like it's mid-pulse. Overlay text as a clean centered stack beneath the waveform. This is the 'aha' moment for the scalability argument.

scenes.title: The Sales Motion: How We Actually Get Clients
scenes.narration: So how do we get clients? Simple outreach. We pick a vertical, find twenty businesses in our area, and send them a free sample ad we made for their exact business. Not a pitch deck — an actual thirty-second ad with their name and their tagline. Most business owners have never heard themselves advertised well. That demo is the sale.
scenes.visual_prompt: Overhead flat-lay composition on a warm dark walnut texture background. A smartphone displays a glowing email or message thread with a short video thumbnail visible — abstract, no readable screen text. Beside it: a small printed business card, a coffee mug with steam, and a simple handwritten note on cream paper. The arrangement is intimate and intentional, like someone crafting a thoughtful outreach at their desk. Warm side lighting from the left. Mood: personal, smart, low-pressure hustle.
scenes.staging_notes: This scene should feel human and approachable — not corporate cold-call energy. The visual metaphor is 'someone doing smart, warm outreach from home.' That's the emotional beat.

scenes.title: Objection: Isn't This Hard to Learn?
scenes.narration: I know what you might be thinking. Is this a whole new skill set? Honestly, the hard part is already done. The voices are trained, the visual templates are built, the pipeline is tested. Your job on a new project is writing a solid brief and pressing go. You don't need to be a sound engineer. You need to know the client.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean editorial split layout on a deep charcoal background. Left side: a chaotic tangle of cables, mixing boards, and studio equipment rendered slightly blurred and desaturated — the 'old hard way.' Right side: a single clean laptop on a minimal desk, glowing softly, with a simple document icon on screen — abstract, no readable text. A bold vertical dividing line in amber separates the two halves. Mood: the contrast between complexity and simplicity. Slightly wry.
scenes.staging_notes: The left side should feel faintly overwhelming; the right side should feel like relief. The arrow labels are the comedy beat — keep them playful. This is the 'I'm answering your real question' scene.

scenes.title: What This Actually Costs to Start
scenes.narration: And the startup cost? Very manageable. We're talking a voice cloning subscription, a Cathode subscription for the video pipeline, and a small budget for outreach samples. All in, you're looking at a few hundred dollars to get a real, sellable product out the door. That's it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Minimal startup cost breakdown motion beat on dark navy background. Three clean line-item rows in a simple table layout. Left column: category label. Right column: estimated cost range. A horizontal rule in teal above a bold total row at the bottom.
scenes.staging_notes: Reveal line items one at a time. The 'Total to Launch' row lands last with a subtle highlight — teal glow or bold weight change. The goal is to make the number feel surprisingly small and completely real.

scenes.title: The Emotional Case: Why This Fits Us
scenes.narration: Here's why I keep coming back to this idea. It fits how we actually work. Creative but structured. Quality-focused. We like making things that feel good. And unlike a lot of side businesses, this one rewards doing great work — because the demo is everything, and great demos spread.
scenes.visual_prompt: Warm intimate scene: a cozy home workspace at dusk. A desk by a window with soft golden-hour light falling across a laptop and a small notepad with loose sketches on it. Two coffee mugs in the frame — implying collaboration. A string of soft Edison bulb lights in the background. No readable text anywhere. The mood is creative, cozy, personal, and full of possibility. Painterly depth of field.
scenes.staging_notes: This is the emotional core scene — the 'this is us' moment. Keep the visual warm and personal, not aspirational in a generic way. It should feel like a real place where real work gets made.

scenes.title: What the First 30 Days Could Look Like
scenes.narration: So here's what a first month could actually look like. Week one: build our voice lineup and produce three sample ads across different verticals. Week two: outreach to forty businesses — twenty personal, twenty via email. Week three: close two to three paid clients. Week four: deliver, get feedback, and build the repeat relationship. That's a real business in thirty days.
scenes.visual_prompt: Four-week timeline motion beat on deep charcoal background. Horizontal timeline bar in teal with four labeled milestone nodes spaced evenly. Each node has a short label below it. Clean, modern, forward-moving energy. No decorative clutter.
scenes.staging_notes: Animate the timeline bar drawing from left to right, then pop each milestone node in sequence with its label. This should feel like a plan being laid out in real time — confident and achievable, not overwhelming.

scenes.title: The Close: This Is the One Worth Trying
scenes.narration: I've looked at a lot of ideas. Most of them require either a huge upfront investment, a skill set we'd have to build from scratch, or clients who are hard to find and harder to keep. This one is different. The market is enormous, the problem is real, the tools are ready, and the barrier to entry is low enough that we can actually start. I think we should.
scenes.visual_prompt: Cinematic wide-angle final frame on a deep midnight blue to near-black gradient background. Centered on screen: a single glowing door, slightly ajar, with warm amber light spilling through the gap onto dark polished floor. The door is clean, modern, and slightly oversized — symbolic rather than literal. Soft atmospheric fog at the floor level. No text in the image. Mood: possibility, calm confidence, invitation. This is the emotional resolution frame.
scenes.staging_notes: This is the landing scene. The door metaphor should feel earned, not cliché — keep it cinematic and tasteful. Overlay the three lines as a stacked, centered fade-in. The narration closes the argument cleanly. Hold the final frame for a full beat before fade to black.
